evidence_quotes.quote: Many apps promise lifetime access, then stop updates or revoke the license.
When servers close, the game vanishes, even if you paid for it.
Every random draw must display clear odds, and the game must show on-screen You have spent X so far.
Dutch gambling rules let problem gamblers block themselves; video games should offer the same.
Every player (or parent) should be able to request a six-month ban on all in-game purchases.
Todays ads follow you across apps and sites, using your data to exploit vulnerabilities.
Targeted ads should be banned or only shown after a clear, separate opt-in.

paragraphs.text: Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act Hello. Im an EU citizen, and I know many of you have never used a computer or smartphone much. You may have family or friends whose children spend hundreds or even thousands of euros on in-game purchases. This can cause real financial harm. Please consider these five simple and clear rules: 1. Clarify and Enforce Lifetime Software Licenses Many apps promise lifetime access, then stop updates or revoke the license. Every lifetime offer must state exactly what it covers (for example, updates until year 2030 or version X only). Companies must be legally bound to honor that promise, with penalties if they break it. 2. Keep Games Playable After Server Shutdown Some games require online servers. When servers close, the game vanishes, even if you paid for it. Ubisofts The Crew lost its offline mode when its servers shut down, leaving buyers with nothing. The law should require every game either to include a true offline mode after shutdown or to let players run private servers. 3. Full Transparency for In-Game Purchases In-game purchases range from cosmetic skins to random draws (gacha). Gacha games like Genshin Impact must already show how much a 5-star character costs and the real-money price of in-game currency (primo gems). Sports titles are even more predatory: FIFA now EA Sports FCreleases almost the same game each year but forces players to buy new card packs. Every random draw must display clear odds, and the game must show on-screen You have spent X so far. 4. Spending Caps & Voluntary Self-Exclusion Ive spent over 10 000 on microtransactions without noticing how fast it adds up, and I know people who faced real harm. Dutch gambling rules let problem gamblers block themselves; video games should offer the same. Every player (or parent) should be able to request a six-month ban on all in-game purchases. For example, Path of Exile support once granted the Reddit user TheRealTuna1 a six-month block after he asked for help with overspending. 5. Restrict Targeted Advertising Todays ads follow you across apps and sites, using your data to exploit vulnerabilities. People recovering from gambling problems, teens, or anyone prone to addiction can be re-triggered by game ads. Targeted ads should be banned or only shown after a clear, separate opt-in. By adopting these five measures, the Digital Fairness Act will make sure Europeans truly own what they pay for, keep software and games accessible, and protect all consumers, especially children and vulnerable adults from aggressive monetization. Thank you for considering my feedback.
